speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Willa Cummings Carlson remembers more of early life on the ridges. Her mother -in -law's midwifing and nursing, preparing for a baby's burial, the naming of American Ridge, a teenager building the first telephone line, and attitudes towards other races, other nationalities, and other towns.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: That's Paul Carlson's mother. Oh, my mother -in -law. Yes. Yes. I wondered if you'd tell me about her midwifing activities and what she was like.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, she made the show you her picture. I've got lots of pictures of her. I haven't seen the picture of her. Well, that might give you some idea. I always think I can get an idea of her picture. I'm not so sure. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I saw the pictures the other week. I did see how it goes. I was thinking you did, of the family. That's right. That's right. She had been, according to Paul's father, and I imagine it was quite true. He said, a beautiful girl when she was young. And I rather think so because even in her old age and she died at, my goodness, I'm not sure, but I believe it was 84. I'm not sure, 84 or 86, but I believe 84. And she even yet had a rosy bloom in her cheek when she was well. She was old and wrinkled. Oh, she was terribly wrinkled. She was wrinkled, but she never lost a fresh complexion. It was strange. She had that. And she had to laugh. She got a great deal out of life. She had to. Paul's father was a solemn old owl, and he didn't watch to cheer her life up. And then she had seven children and one child to die. And it was 16 months old. And they moved to the farm. She didn't have sard, of course, when they were in town. She always raised a garden, I guess, when she was in town. But they didn't have sard in town. But when she got to the farm, oh my, and she went right, she had lived in a farm in Sweden and she knew about how to milk. Well, believe me, he never milked a cow. And he'd even drive it up to the porch so she could milk it when she was sick. The cow, I mean, drive the cow up to the porch. And he did that a few times, but usually he could get a man that milked. They didn't, if he had a man there, even in Sweden, they didn't want to see a woman milking so they'd milk the cow. But that's the way he was. But she was a worker and she did an awful lot of work on that farm and a good housekeeper. And people liked her. She never learned to handle the English language proficiently, but she handled it fast. But it was always broken, Swedish. And people liked her. And everybody on Burnt Ridge sang her praises. And a lot of them did not like Mr. Carlson. Of course, he always prided himself on, he didn't care what he was like tonight, he wasn't to be, and he wanted people to know that he was just. He wanted them to know he was just in his decisions. Well, no matter what you thought, whether you thought that he was always just or not, he always advertised that he was. And that he was fair -minded. And I believe that the people thought that he was fair because they would oftentimes ask for his advice in settling disputes on the ridge. Oh, I suppose you'd call them small disputes, but the disputes that will come up in a neighborhood where they own a thrashing machine together and work together for the good of the community. And they did that on Burnt Ridge. Wonderful people on Burnt Ridge. I never lived among nicer people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember any of his decisions or any of the problems that he made?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know, I can remember them in a way, but I really don't want to go into my end of all those problems.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, I was thinking, no, what I meant was any decisions that he made as a judging in disputes, I think that's interesting.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, no, because I didn't pay enough attention to give them accurately. If I gave anything like that, I'd want it to absolutely be accurate. And he would be accurate if he tried to tell when I think I'd give him that. I think that he would be. And so I wouldn't want to detail anything without having all the facts before me and I. And I didn't have them before me that plainly because I got so I didn't listen to him when he'd be telling me. That's one of the reasons why I couldn't really tell. What about Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carlson's reputation as a midwife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, they couldn't always get a doctor out there. Their owns were muddy and one thing or another. And now here's another thing that I don't know. Well, economy was a factor. Doctors cost money. And if they could get somebody to do the work, well, they'd get them. And of course, she never thought of charging them anything. But they always, they remembered her and they would try to do things for her. She'd get many things done for her in the way of gifts and things like that. Something that they could give. And she would get things from them for helping them out. And they would come to get her and tell her that so and so was sick. Or they, instead of having a nurse, they didn't get a nurse at all. They'd have her, she might, even if they had a doctor. They'd have a doctor and call him Mrs. Carlson. In fact, the doctor got used to having her places. I don't know. I remember her daughter, Mrs. Duthie, Ellen, counting up one time how many places mom had been. She said, I just counted up how many babies mom would bring into the world. She's practically brought in this generation, she said, when she was talking. But I don't remember the figure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you know what seems to me like she said 22 babies at that time?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But I'd better not give that as a figure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any times that stand out when she was called on to be a midwife?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: The Lawrence Dawson children, after she went to the ridge from Moscow, she was up there when they were all born. I know that, that Lawrence Dawson had come down for her and asked her to come. And Paul's father got so he complained about it that she'd go off and leave him. My goodness, he had to get a meal, had to get a breakfast sometimes. But he'd get the kids out. They had to get out. They learned to get breakfast pretty quickly. And he'd have to get them up. But they had no idea how they inconvenienced him. Not to say anything at all about how the inconvenience turned. He never mentioned that. She was inconvenienced or anything of that sort. But that because she'd left, why poor old me, I had to suffer. That's the way he thought about it. I heard a lot of that. But there was a family that lived down in the canyon by the name of Moody. And down the canyon she'd have to go down to the railroad track, cross the railroad track, and then halfway up the canyon on the other side where this bench was. And there this cabin was built. And I don't know how it came that came about that old Pete Moody bought that cabin. But he bought it. And they had several small children. And she went down there when they had a baby. And then a little later on they drank polluted water from some stream around there. Of course, you know, there was cattle running in the canyon and everything else. And they got typhoid fever. Two of the children and one of them died, it was a little girl. And I remember going to that funeral because I was in Sunday school and they said they were going to have a funeral for the little girl. And some of the superintendents of Sunday school asked all the little girls that were anywhere near that girl's age, she was younger than I quite a little bit, that they would all sit up front and sing the songs that they'd sung for Children's Day. That's what we did. And they had to carry that casket out of the house. I don't know how old she was, but I think maybe about nine or ten.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And then the boy was sick and else was wounded. And they used to want her to come and help take care of him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know why.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: The cousin of the mother was sick I guess. But she'd been over there helping take care of that typhoid child. And there were two children that had it. And she'd go walk over there in the morning and get up and do up her work. Hurry up. Walk down that canyon, up that side, the only other side, while I was just given out if I walked across the canyon. Believe me, I couldn't do anything more until I got a rest. I had to have a rest. That was just enough for me. It was a day's work and I used to think that I was doing too much by jumping in and doing anything because I didn't get to rest too long. But my goodness, I couldn't do like she did. Oh, she was a strong Husky woman and she could do that. And then she'd go back, carry water up the hill from the spring. They told me she wasn't doing that when I knew her because they had the, as I told you, they put that hydraulic ram into this wonderful spring. By that time they had the water pumped by the ram up on the hill. So she didn't have to go and carry water then. But they said that she did when she was going to Moody's. She'd come back, she'd leave her buckets there and she'd carry water up the hill and then get home and milk the cows, take care of the milk and it's supper and everything else.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She did it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, she was a worker. And I could never hope to work with her. I know I did an awful big day's work after I was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm going to ask an ignorant question of my part, which is, why would she be so sought after? What makes her a real midwife as compared to just a woman helping at the birth of a child?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, I gave her the name of midwife. I think she'd hesitate to have it that way. But she wasn't afraid of anything. She was a dauntless woman. I don't believe there was a thing that she was afraid of. And if a doctor once told her what to do and she went someplace, she would apply that again. And still she could get panicky about something. So I don't know that she was. She wasn't a trained midwife, only she'd gotten a good training by being called out so many times to ask. I think because in that day and age, it was rare to find a woman who was willing to do it. And she went because they asked her because there was a need and she tried to fill it. And she filled it so adequately so many times that finally she became recognized for having done it. Now, that's my explanation of that, because she wasn't a trained midwife. She was trained on a few things that most women weren't. And then she had a naturally even nice disposition. And for others, I think she was kind. I thought there were times when I didn't see this kindness. I knew her as a warm human being who did an awful lot of good. And certainly I knew more good about her than I did anything else. But I also knew her as a human being who had the frailties and the faults along with the good points of humankind. Now, that's the way I knew her. Now, her daughters deified her.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Do you know or know of what, around the birth, what practices were people followed to make sure that

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: you know what I'm driving at?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, she knew all about hot water and boiling everything that touched the patient and the clean sheets. And those Swedish women were clean. All of them were clean. They knew about cleanliness. They were cleaner than the American women that I knew I'm saying an awful thing. Don't for heaven's sake put that in there. Money will be mud. But they were so clean and they understood the principle of cleanliness. And they scrubbed and scoured. And they were much more particular about their scrubbing and scouring, I think, than the ordinary woman. And so they would be clean. And Paul's mother was especially so. She was very, very clean. And she had cleanliness. And everything that touched the patient would be clean and be scoured. But beyond that, I don't know what she had.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: You said before that she believed in the old wives' tales and the old ways. You didn't use the word old wives' tales, but that's sort of what I draw from it. That there were old beliefs that she had, such as the case where the young girl was buried, where she buried alive. That's sort of what we might call superstitions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there beliefs at first that you can think of that are those old country beliefs that were brought over here?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No. I don't know.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I noticed that her daughter, Ellen, when she was going to have her children, that she didn't have her mother. And of course, when Paul Jr. was to be born, she thought I should stay with her in Lewiston. And I just didn't want to. And I went to the hospital, but I had a very bad time. Things were not right. And I always thought it was a blessing I was at a hospital considering what had happened. But she said, if I'd have been at home, that those things wouldn't have come up. That was her attitude. I never knew so much about that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: However, I didn't get to the hospital. I had a bad weather and an awful bad time. When Bruce was born, and I was in the country and wasn't prepared for this at all, but when it came along, I did have one awful good neighbor. And I called her, and she came down to help the doctor. We had one terribly good doctor, and he told Paul what to get in town. And so we had all the disinfectants and everything that he could possibly want or need. And he came in the door scolding, I sinned. I think I hadn't gotten out of there. But that was over in Kooski.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: There's this little mention of her when they came to a ridge. It was said that she took flowers from her hat when there were no fresh flowers when a baby had died to put in a casket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that about her?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: No, I never did hear that. I didn't know that she did. She would do it if they didn't have any, and she might have done it. I wouldn't say she didn't do it, but I did not know anything about it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Now I'll tell you, I went through, I'm not saying I went through anything like she did, but I mean that Harris Ridge, where we lived over in Kooski in Idaho County, was so primitive compared to Burnt Ridge that you'd have thought you'd gotten into that primitive region for sure. By the time I came to live on Burnt Ridge, and of course she had lived there for some years before that, and it wasn't as settled up and as good. When she went out there, she went out there when it was worse. But oh, they went out there, let's see, they built that house in 1915, and they lived in the old house, I believe five years before they got that, and got the ram in.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But all the children were born in Moscow, except the little one that died, the little girl that died. She was born after they went to the farm.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She died in measles.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But it was not so primitive. My goodness, I thought I'd had a primitive. I found what I'd complained about what my folks had, foolish, compared to what I found over there. And because I went in as a school teacher, people thought I should know. I had never officiated at a death. There were lots of older women. My mother might go to someplace, and she didn't go much. My mother didn't go out much, but she did some. Somebody was sick and dying. She felt she didn't go on help, and she did. But when I got to Burjrit, I was a school teacher, and I was called on. I never will forget the first time I was called on. A young woman married, and she had this baby prematurely, and it died. Well, that mother was a midwife, and advertised herself to be from Finland, and said she had had training in Finland. There's a bunch of Finlanders up there, never find people. But no thanks. I didn't want them for midwives. Not with me. But nevertheless, Rita didn't get out of there. Oh, the roads are terrible. And they didn't get the doctor there in time. The baby was dead. And they asked the doctor, no, he said it's a pretty big baby and all that, but he said it hadn't matured. It was just too young. And if we'd have had it, we could put it in an incubator and all those things. It might have lived, but it was dead. Well, she was among Finlanders, and she wasn't used to it. And I represented them as school teachers, ought to know. And to her I represented somebody like she. And I was an American girl, an American woman. I was just a young woman, mind you, and I'd never taken care of it. Yes, I'd taken care of a baby where a woman needed to have somebody take care of the baby after it was born and all that, but I didn't have anything to do with the birth. And so I went and I prepared that baby for burial. And believe it or not, it wasn't easy. But I had this one blessed neighbor, oh, she was good. And she said they didn't ask me, but she said, I think you need company. You go over there tonight.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She said, don't you, do you want me along?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I said, oh, yes, I certainly do. She was the one I had to come when Bruce was born. So she came down, we went horseback. Oh, the roads are terrible. And Mavis was a baby. Paul could stay home with the baby, but he couldn't do anything else. And he gave me all kinds of instructions and all kinds of warnings. I don't know why all that. I went over there and they'd gone to town. Two of them had gone horseback, but they'd have to bring anything from town that they needed. And they had a number of things to bring. So they weren't going to get a casket and the crap under was there. He would make it. And he says, I've got the boards out here all flamed and nice. He says, they're beautiful sanded and they're white and they're beautiful boards.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: He said, would you tell me how to make it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, Mrs. Fitting and I, those had the same idea. He might make it a plain one, but one was slanted, you know, sloped on the head. Smart. And he did a beautiful job on that. He really did. Of course, he was a character. Lived up there in that country. No bachelor named Dietrich. Never forget him. And he said, well, no, that casket's good enough for anybody. Well, then they sent to town by her stepfather, his stepfather, stepfather, the man she married, for something to line it with. Well, he brought up some cotton with nothing else. And I said, well, I've got something. I was thinking we could dress up in front of the Mavis out of it later on. Somebody giving it to us for Christmas for Mavis, I think. Yard and a half of Noah is something else. But anyways, yard and a half of material. I said, I've got something over home. And I've got lace. The lace was intended for a dress. Putting things around. Brothers, they called it a lace. I went home and got them and made that. Mrs. Fitting and I fitted it in and sewed it in. The line to put. And then, oh no, Mrs. Line, they said, I'm not going to put the cotton. Go in there. We don't need cotton, she said. And they had sheeps wool as she'd washed and was snowy white.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They had sheeps.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And she carded that wool. She carded it so fast and laid that carded wool in there, so nice and white and fluffy. And then we put the lining in, Mrs. Fitting and I, and fitted it in there. We really did that. We did it all. We got the little thing ready and made the casket. Behind the casket and took care of that. And then we wondered what we'd do for flowers. And I said, well, they had some tender young spruce down underneath. And we had the boys cut some of the real tender spruce. I took a sack of that home. A flower sack full. And I made little reeds after I got home. I said, I've got ribbons. And I'll tie ribbons on it. And my Christmas cactus, it had been Paul's mother's. Great big Christmas cactus was in bloom. And there were 21 blooms. And they went around on that. Three of the reeds had three of the prettiest reeds yourself. Ribbons and those pink cactuses. So when you mention putting flowers on something, now that's what Paul's mother would have done. And so no doubt she did that. And they evidently knew something about it. Now you see, Jack Driscoll, Paul's father, bought the original Carlson Place, the original 160. He bought it as a preemption from Jack Driscoll, one of the Driscoll's. And at that time, that was before I went over there, now they were having babies in and there were people that left the ridge before I ever got there. And it might have been one of those. And he probably went as one of the men to take care of things and maybe saw all that. And that would have been just the kind of a thing she'd done. And I expect she did. I wouldn't be at all surprised. But I didn't happen to see it or know about that. But we did this for the baby. And of course the mother, you know, was overwhelmed because she wanted something done for her. And she had felt so lonely and so alone. And that was up there. And that was quite a primitive place, believe me. Oh, Mrs. Fitting and I went on a lot of errands two or three times on horseback. When we got together, we'd always laugh and talk about the places that we'd been to. But now that's not Leitel County.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: What I wanted to ask you about was, we touched on it a couple of few times, but it really gets down to, I think something very important is what was the difference in character among different nationalities and the different ridges and different places. And I'm wondering if, you know, what differences the cultural backgrounds did make to, let's say, Swedes, Irish people as compared to each other, Norwegian people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were some of the differences?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: There weren't so many differences and the differences were soon obliterated. It didn't take long. After, it was like the Bostonians, you know, the ones that lived up on the hill, the Shanty Irish, as they were called, and they came over. Well, they soon found out that the Irish, those Shanty Irish, could live in those big fine homes back there just as well as the rich something else's. I don't know what they were, but the haughty English that had to have so much. So just about the same, the differences melted away. There was the language. At first there was the language barrier. And of course they spent a lot of time trying to imitate the other fellow the way he taught. Some time was given to that, that this Swede said that when he went in where the butcher was. And little things that they might think was a difference. I know there are little things that are differences, little things in what you keep.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And,

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: oh, they were all so small that I guess I've forgotten them long ago. But the way things were cooked and the way things were served, that served as a medium of conversation for a while, you know, among them. But then they began to taste things and they found that the Swedes were awful good cooks. And pretty soon the young folks were going over because they had such good feed was over there. And they ate so many cookies. Well, they never saw so many cookies in all their lives. But they were such good cookies. Get the recipe, all right. And pretty soon the English were making the Swedish cookies and some were the others. Norwegians were making this, that or the other. Ludifisk, they were cooking Ludifisk at Christmas time. What the dickens is Ludifisk. Some of these people have never heard of it. Well, it's good. We'll find out how they make it and we'll make some. Pretty soon they were having Ludifisk at Christmas time. And now, a patati scurve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what it is?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, it's really pork sausage. With some beef mixed in, I guess. Any way they want it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Quite lean.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And they, it's ground very fine and put through the grout. Since I've helped to make this, I have helped my mother -in -law make it. And she always wanted the meat, the sausage, ground very fine. And then that and potatoes and the seasonings that were put in. There were seasonings put in, of course, lots of pepper and maybe red pepper and, uh, I don't remember whether any other spices are not all spice, I think. She was always particular about the spice that were put in. You used all spice with meats, but you didn't use all spice with fruits and things like that because they did use lots of spice. And I'll tell you these. Then they stuff them and they used to make, they got a little instrument. You buy them at the hardware store, at a hardware store. And those are what you stuff the sausages with. And they are the intestines, of course. That they clean out. They, uh, and it's not near as bad to clean out. Oh, I thought, at first, we all had our noses turned up because we couldn't possibly eat anything that was inside that. Never thinking about all the winners and things of that sort that were being consumed, you know. And then when they found that out, of course, it wasn't bad then. But then, uh, how they could clean them and used to clean them at home. My mother knew how to clean them and put them on this thing and then just roll back the intestine and it was clean and tear it out and tear out the inside. And then nothing could be cleaner. And they were soaked in salt water for so long a time and then washed and dried. And then they were, and then if you, a little time elapsed before you, you, uh, used them, you had to soak them again so they'd be wet and you could get them out. And then you'd stuff that with a sausage. And that's Patati's Curve. P -O -T -A -T -I -S.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Patati's.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: M -K -E -R -V, I think it is. And it's very good. And that's what made every Christmas in every Swedish home. And my, it wasn't long till every English home having Patati's Curve too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the Irish from Greskal Ridge and the Swedish people from the nearby areas hit it off at the beginning?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They never had any show. But the Swedes and the Irish, and the Irish took in everything that was American. Otherwise it had been there for a year or two, you know. The Irish didn't have enough men to fill out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had a, a, um, oh, what do you call it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Tug -a -war.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: See which could out pull the other. And those Swedish lumberjacks, you know, they could usually pull pretty hard. But of course there were lots of Irish lumberjacks too, you know. They were back to Paul Bunyan. And they'd take those brawny Irishmen and it was quite a thing to see which one could, the only other thing they always called it, the Swedes against the Irish. But the Irish cheated. They had everything in the way of a big man. Because of course they'd been here a long time and could talk the language.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this just on celebration days?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Celebration days, yes. On celebration days. That was all. And, oh, sometimes they'd hear about fights they'd get into. More or less good natured when they got started getting to them. Sometimes they'd get pretty rough. But there was always somebody around to bring them into order. And I never heard of anybody getting seriously hurt or anything on a fun day.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I know that in a lot of pioneering areas, those people who are English speaking often tend to regard themselves or be regarded as superior in the beginning because the foreigners can't speak the language. Oh yes, in the beginning, like Walmer.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: You know, the ones who couldn't say Walmer. Or, look down upon them as though there was something the matter with them. In a way, you might say. But believe me, wait until the next generation comes up and you don't. Oh yes, even as late as when I started teaching. Not started teaching, after I taught them and going back teaching made it worse. There were a couple of children who had been taught Swedish in a Swedish home because they thought that when they went to school they would lose it because they're always dead. Now, Paula never said a word of English until his brother and sister, just older, went to school and they came home and they told him a lot of words to say so he wouldn't be left out in the cold. They didn't want him to be regarded as too heathenish. So he knew a few words and he went to school. But they'd never talk English at home. They talked Swedish. And they were much better than the younger children but when they went out on the farm, Paula's father and mother, he tried to keep her talking and she talked broken English. That wasn't good at all. But he talked pretty good English. But they talked Swedish in the home all the time because they wanted the children to learn Swedish. Which is a good thing. I'm all for it. And people came to me, never liked to tell this because it actually happened. And said, now you just get after those kids. You talk to those parents too. You tell them you don't want them talking Swedish to them at home. And they've got to learn to talk like these other kids. And don't give them, just see to us that they learn it. Don't give them grades that you think they might have gotten or anything like that. But you agree to exactly what they taught them. If they don't talk English, don't consider they've done anything. I had several people do. As late as that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think they were, what's behind their doing that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They wanted everybody to talk English. When I went over to Kooski, same thing over there. Idaho County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Little fellow askers.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Crazy guy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: This is a fair place. Right in the beginning before I ever taught a day of school. But anyway I was going to teach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are you going to do about these people that talk Finnish? If they don't like what the teacher says, will they start talking to each other in Finnish?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They said, or anything comes up. Or in class. Right out in class. They'll break out talking Finnish. And then they'll laugh. I said that's the way they've been doing the teachers.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: What are you going to do about that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I want to see.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Some teacher can break them. Well I said it doesn't worry me a bit. Not a bit. They thought I was funny. No. I said I think it's interesting. I've never been in a Finnish neighborhood before and I intend to learn Finnish. So I said well I'm so glad that you people already know a language up here. And about half of them were Finnish. But there was a good half that wasn't. I said I'm so glad that you know a language. You know you're so much ahead of all the others that still have all their language learning to do. I said I've always wanted to know about the Finnish. I said I've looked up a lot of things about Finnish and you've got lots to be proud of. About those old Finnish patriarchs and how they were subdued you might say by Sweden and still they never gave up their independence. They remained independent through it all. And I even oh I traced a Finnish history. I just took it up big. What about the Norwegians which we had in Laitau County.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have a reputation for being especially hard workers Norwegians out in the dairy area?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I think so. Those woodsmen whether they were Norwegian or Swedish I don't think they were any worse than the Swedes but all the Scandinavians. Truth of the matter is lots of people used to stop and say oh you don't mean Norwegians or Swedes you mean Scandinavians. And another thing that I thought about afterwards that I didn't call your attention to but that I thought was interesting and informative that when they went to name American Ridge a lot of different names were suggested. They named for their leaders. Bill Hart had called them out here. Bill Hart Ridge and this Ridge and that Ridge. There were quite a number of names suggested. And Mrs. Roberts said and of course Roberts's were here then. And I believe that they said it was Mrs. Roberts but at least someone among them said well why not. Oh yes and then there was such a great big lot of people in the upper end of the ridge not the lore that came from Indiana. And they said well we might call it Indiana Ridge. There's such a big settlement here of Indiana. And then a big settlement of the people from Ohio. Well that was hardly fair to them. And they thought about that section of the country but nobody really wanted to do that either. But then there was a bunch from Kansas and they said what could they call that. It was kind of like Kansas region after all Bill Hart who they suggested to name it in the first place had come from Kansas. And then somebody says well why not. Call it American Ridge because no matter what else we are we're Kansans and Ohioans and we've even got a German or two among us and we've got all these different people among us. And they were practically casting the German and the Hollander they were practically classing them as you know just as Ohioans and Kansans really. And they said why should we name it for any group. We're all kinds of people but first of all and first and foremost we are Americans. Americans all. So why not call it American Ridge. And that caught on at once. When they named it American Ridge and they had a flag raising and I thought that was interesting. You feel that there was a usually high acceptance of whatever country that people came from. On American Ridge yes. It was. And that was on the lower end of American Ridge or well up toward the middle of getting up pre -coast where I was. And the people who came in for the North of that where they were all. Well they were just in there from Kansas and Arkansas. There were some from Arkansas. They were up there but it was Indiana and Ohio and Illinois Pennsylvania a few from New York and people in there not very many in the first place from New England. After a while we hear about somebody coming from the ring but there weren't so many from New England in there in the beginning. And I think that they generally did accept it. That's what they went out there. They were going to be people from everywhere.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: What about the. I've heard that the people from Dry Creek and people around Nora had a really tough reputation. Is that true when you were young.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well not so much when I was young but yes I would have been pretty young. I would have been too young to know much about it. Now Nora had a rough reputation but when I hear about the rough reputation of the early days it was fighting. And drinking but my goodness it couldn't match the immorality of the present times. That's what I always come back with. You're a nice moral lot to be talking about those people. But yes Nora you know where that was. That's a little place that was bypassed and now has gone forever. And it was a it was a post office there. There was a post office there I should say. And there were great times there and there was a kind of a dance all there in this building that they put up where they had dances. And so I believe there were three saloons I think I heard. I don't know why they had that. But there were. Lumber Jackson this neighborhood and Lumber Jackson were another part of the woods and Lumber Jackson were another and then they'd have fights. See which were the best ones. I think they probably were fairly good nature fights in the beginning but of course they get mad as they fight and naturally after they get the fire water into them what they had there because it wasn't I'm sure it wasn't a very good great a whiskey. And oh they said there were. And now that's what I know my father didn't drink and he didn't go out among them and he never went up there and I never heard anything at home. I never learned anything about Nora. I'd hear once in a while Mr. Roberts tell well they had a great fight up there and two or three fellas got cut up cut to pieces. They had him in for Dr. Scallon to sew up. I never heard of anybody getting killed but I did hear about him being sold out. And that it was an awful place but I never heard it at home you see so it was a little different but for my husband's people who were Swedes themselves and believe me Paul's father was keeping track of the Swedes and what they did and he'd of horsewhip to boys I suppose if any of them had shown up up there and then I'm sure after they got a little older I'm sure they did go out there and see what was going on. They didn't participate in the fights they did. I don't suppose they'd take all that but they didn't know a lot more about it and I heard a lot more about it from them. And Paul said it was a rousing place. They'd just tear up everything at Nora every now and then. And then they finally got to sending the law out there the marshal of somebody else. I don't know whether old man Hayes ever went out there enough to try to keep order. I don't know about that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I also heard that for some reason the girls from Viola were not held in very high repute around Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there any truth to that?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But I don't think that the girls meant any worse than the boys. I never heard that but that the people that were out in Viola were of a different stamp and that they weren't such good people. Now I used to hear that and they always had a man. I'll tell you it carried. Now I was too young. You see as I said I wasn't brought up in a religious home but I was brought up in a strictly temperance home. And my father had no use for riotous living of any kind. Not any kind. And I would hear things but that's all. The Harlan family who lived near us I heard most of everything that I heard from them and they weren't allowed to go up to Nora. I'll tell you that. Raymond and Leonard. And they would break out as much as any boys around in the community. And I used to get the word from Raymond and then I never heard from Raymond about he thought that these vigilantes had formed a group around there. I never heard that from him until. Well after I was thinking about writing a paper on it and I said say tell me what you know about about this. But he never got to know anything about firsthand at least about Nora and up there. He'd hear from others. But Viola they used to say well it's a cesspool of the country. All the roughnecks go to Viola. And it had a rough reputation. And then Nora didn't help at any. He was right down there by it practically. And all the roughnecks went there. There were plenty of rough swings too. And rough Norwegians. But they weren't that at home. They lived differently when they got out. But anyway I wondered about that. But as late as when I came in to teach in Moscow. We none of us wanted a school that's predominantly made up of Viola kids.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They had run teachers out of the school out there and they had a rural school. Of course you see these schools were all reorganized by the time I taught in here. But there had been years when they were rural schools. And they'd run teachers out of the schools and they had an awful time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Bovill? Did that have any kind of reputation?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know about Bovill. Of course they had lumberjacks and lumberjacks are always. They always get a reputation of drinking and noise making and things of that sort. And I don't know how bad they are. But no, Bovill, Lord Bovill came in there you know. And that was interesting. Very interesting. I used to have my years open about that. He was supposed to be a younger man. A younger member of one of the families. They're not royalty. Next to royalty. The family is under royalty, whatever they call them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: The Lords.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They are the Lords, yes. But they have a name for that class of society. But never having been in that class of society, I only know about it from reading but I used to read lots about it. And so the set that was just under royalty was always doing their things. They were nearly always, once in a while, royalty would escape down into that group and get out through them. Well anyway, he was one of the younger sons that didn't get, he was over here. That's what I heard. And so they said that this man that came in, they didn't look like anything different to any other man than me. He was just a common Lord and a man to me. When I saw it, they said this was Lord Bovill. I was terribly anxious to see him. Lord. I thought he'd look something like Lord. That was my definition of Lord. I didn't have the right one. I looked back upon my ignorance. I know there were many things of which I was ignorant. But I'd learned that I was ignorant and I did learn one thing. That I could keep my mouth shut. And as soon as I got so I could look in a dictionary, that I could write down the word as I thought it was spelled. And I could look it up in the dictionary. And we didn't have a dictionary at first. And when I got to school, as soon as I was through with my studies and it didn't take me long, I studied the dictionary. And I learned from the dictionary. And I learned what things were. I learned the definitions. And I learned a lot like that. And I know why I said that Lord Bovill doesn't look any different to anybody else. And Papa laughed and he said, no, of course not. He said he's just a man. That's a name they give him. But over there they have titles. And they give men and women titles. And we don't here know only title you had here is Mr. and Mrs.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And so as far as that was concerned, I didn't know anything about him. But he would come and he did drive that team of spanking black horses. Oh, they were beauties. High stepping and all are out on Burnt Ridge. But that team was getting older. Goody loaded. And he drove it to a buggy. But he also drove it to a Surrey. And he used to come into Troy every Sunday because his wife died and his wife now was a sister to Mr. Kelberg. So the others and Kelbergs were related and lived right down there together. And they were both forward looking men. I always thought that, well, I guess that's, no, I won't say that because I wouldn't want that to go down in his trade because maybe I would be wrong. But anyway, there was quite a bit of rivalry.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But Mr. Kelberg and Mr. Otter were both forward looking men. And they wanted the rural free mail delivery to come in. And they wanted the telephones. And they worked for telephones a lot. The telephone got in on American Ridge in 1909. They put a telephone line through to Lewiston. But of course they didn't have any telephones on it. But in a few years they did put on a trance, well, these substations. And a few people got on. But I remember it was 1909 when the first telephone line went through. And it was just a few years then until we got the line through. And then I've got somewhere in my notes I've got the time, the year when the free delivery was put in too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that story in your notes about the phone that that young fella put in who came out?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh yes, I think I gave that quite a bit of space because we were terribly interested in that. You did, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you watch and build that, put that line together?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Oh, a little of it. I didn't see it. No, Mama wouldn't let me. She wouldn't let me. She thought it was terrible. And she always ran to Papa with that story that I was out. But Arville and I, my little brother, he was little. He's four years younger than I. But we used to run out and go to the C Ford whenever we could. He was about 16 or 17. It was all. And he was in high school. My was an interesting kid. And we just thought the world of Ford. Noah believed that last summer he was out. He was 18. And he told us it was not very long ago he was going to get out and do things. And my was going to do the last. Well he went. He came from Chicago but he didn't go to Berkeley. And as far as I know where he went. He went to Berkeley and he was a scientist. And he did get a lot of honors down there. I am sure Ford made his way in the world. Because he was a smart kid. But this particular situation. In this situation he got together some wire. He had to buy some of it. He had money of course. And this doctor who always came out to hunt was Ford's uncle. And that's how he got out there. That uncle brought him. I think Ford's people were dead. At least his mother was dead. And he had a cigar box. He had cigar boxes. And he cut a little hole in them. And he had paint. And he said no he was going to paint over this stuff. I don't know what he had but he had wire netting of some kind. But it was heavy. And he cut around it. He had some tools along with him. And he cut around that and put a little hole in it. And that was the transmitter. And then after you tapped it with a piece of strap iron. And you heard somebody say hello. Had your ear up close. Then you come up with hello and gave your message. And said no. Now you tell and you say you answer me. And then you put your ear to it. You both talked and heard over the same thing. That was lots of fun. You got to do it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: For neighbors.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But I didn't get to see much of it. Because Mama wasn't going to have me down there either bothering Ford or playing with Ford. She didn't trust these kids from the city. And Ford was just as nice as he could be. What a nice kid he was. I was just a little child and little kid. But I knew Ford was alright. I'd been back in 1945. Six of the folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he go from? The home that he spent?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, over on American Age he went from the Roberts house where Ann Roberts lived. Up on the hill opposite to the way we went from there. Just up on the hill above them. To their daughters. Ressie who was married to Dan Helm. And that was where the Dan Helm said. But he had to raise it. It was raised through quite a bit of space to go up on the hill. And he was always figuring that. And oh he let me hold the stake that he was measuring. And I was so proud of it. I was going to go back to Chicago and study science with Ford. That's what I was going to do. I was going to hurry up and get through the eighth grade. You hurry up and get through the eighth grade. He said we'll take our degrees in science. That's what we'll do. And he was always bragging on me and telling me what a lot I was. And he told me so much. The folks never understood that I was getting so much from that boy. Told us all kinds of things. And Orville, I think he learned more from him than he ever did from a school teacher. Because Orville didn't care. He didn't give a hoot when he learned anything or not. Only this was easy stuff. He was just getting there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of stuff was he teaching you? About science?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: All about science and about what you could do with wires and what you could do with telegraphy and telephone work. This was telephone. He'd tell about the telegraph and how the country would be connected. After a while we'd have pictures. Some things that I haven't seen yet that we'd have television on the telephone. You'd take up the receiver and people could see them whether they were talking to the right person or not. He was on a fire with that. The fact was going to be soon. He expected to see that in his day but I guess he never got around to it. I don't know what became of Ford. I'd like to know what became of that kid. But I know he must have gone places. Maybe he got killed in the war. Bright -headed. He didn't go down. But old Doc Whitaker thought he was smart. He was educating you. And he thought a lot of him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Last time we talked a lot about the attitudes that people on the rich had towards the Negroes. I realized after that I wanted to ask you also briefly what the attitudes were that they had towards the Indians and Chinese.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they the same as the attitude towards Negroes?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: They didn't have the same attitude toward Indians.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Of course my mother was scared of the death of Indians. She'd come out from Missouri. She had lived in Missouri for some years. She was born in Ohio but she lived there. And back there they were scared of Indians. They weren't afraid of Negroes. They put them down. But they were scared of Indians. I know that she, of course there was wrong psychology and wrong from every point of view, but my mother didn't have any psychology and I doubt she'd have used it if she'd had it. But she used to tell me that the Indians picked up bad kids now. Be careful because the Indians did come through every now and then. She had me afraid of Indians too. I was afraid when I saw them coming I got to the house if I was out someplace. I didn't want to be caught out with these Indians. Until a family, a mocks mocks family, five or six Indians came and stopped at Mrs. Roberts's when I was there. And she said, you come on out with me and we'll see them. And they wanted to give her some beads or something for it. And she didn't want to make them mad so they did. They made a trade with Mrs. Roberts. And Mrs. Roberts talked to them and just as big as you please. And they told her all kinds of things, confidential things like Pete mocks mocks had beaten his wife and another mocks mocks had done something that he oughtn't to do. And then they laughed. And they were going cranberrying. They were going up in the hills to go cranberrying. And they knew a place up on Moscow Mountain where they could get wonderful cranberries. They knew how to get them. And oh my, I made a great fuss about that. And got back inside the fence.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And Mrs. Roberts said, oh they're just teasing you. They're just teasing you. That's a good thing. She said that that shakes off all the dust. We have to shake off the dust. She was a wise woman and she also knew a lot of human psychology. I don't know how much she knew from books but she knew worlds from human beings.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Wonderful woman. I loved her.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: So you had a pretty good impression of the Indians when you met them. Oh yes, and I never was afraid of them again. I never was afraid of the Indians again. But I will tell you though what happened to me growing up in just a few years ago. So it was after I was a student teaching here and I taught here until I was seven and I'm 78 now and then I went to Boston and taught, well I lived down there three and a half years. And for two years I taught in Head Start and this was after I was teaching in Head Start I think. But anyway, an Indian family moved in across the way and they said that my brother -in -law, my sister lived down there too, they were very close to me and I used to hand throw away. And my brother -in -law told me that, well his name was, I can't remember what his name was, no, Shucks. I wonder if I've forgotten that Indian family's name. They had five children and he was at the head of the school for, Head Start school for the Indians at Flat Point. I was living in Austin and I was wishing I could get acquainted with him and learn something about the Indians. I just figured I was going to learn all kinds of things about him because the wife didn't look too often friendly and she wasn't going to act as if she was terribly friendly, a proud woman. But I thought it would be so nice if I could get acquainted with him but I knew she didn't want to just accept any old kind of getting acquainted and I wanted to get acquainted with him. Well one night, one Sunday afternoon, I'd been lonesome all day and I'd written letters all afternoon and in the evening I was awful tired but I had a good air conditioner so I wasn't roasting like most people were and nearly everybody was outside. I didn't need to go out and cool off because I have a good air conditioner in this trailer. So I had a great big stack of letters I had ready to mail. And I went out to mail them in the mailboxes for the people across the street and the other side of the street. And there were only three of us in that trailer park but right down below were two of us so I made five mailboxes there and I don't know about five on the other side, ten mailboxes, regular town mailboxes. And I went out there to mail them and I put my letters in the box and the flag up and then I stood on to look at a sunset because no place has prior sunsets in Idaho and I just love that sunset. And of course we've got a lot of dust in the air and that's what helps make them beautiful. But I was looking at that gorgeous sunset and enjoying it all by myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I just got lost in it and all at once a big heavy voice said, hot enough for you?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I looked up and I see a great big Indian brave dressed in Bermuda shorts and barelegged barefooted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what did I do?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I screamed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I was so mad at myself. Oh I was mad at myself. As soon as I got over that I said, oh he says, oh excuse me. And my goodness he paddled off to the house so fast you know you can, faster than I can say Jack Robinson. He went into the house. Oh my goodness. I was so mad at myself. Here I had been wanting to get acquainted with that family and I queered it that way.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Thank goodness. Let me ask you about the Chinese now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the attitudes towards the Chinese like the attitudes towards the Negroes?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well there weren't enough here.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: When one came in they treated him as a sort of a, I don't know, a sort of a, well I don't know, just like a something entirely different. And so everybody sort of noticed him and noticing him. They'd speak to him and he was always nice and polite and very polite and minded his own business. And I never heard anybody say anything about the Chinese. I read about in the different places in the mining camps and lumber camps before he went. They called him a heathen Chinese and a lot of that you know. And some would like him but most of them seemed to make fun of him and so on. And here you'd hear every now and then somebody well quoting him and trying to talk like he did. Now for instance I did that. I spoke to him to see my grandmother and I used to do it in the summertime. And there was a Chinese gardener laid right down below her and he knew her. He got into nowhere and grandma knew him. She knew enough about Chinese. And my grandmother was a wise woman too by the way. And she knew how to handle people. Nobody ever ever mistrusted my grandmother or ever mistreated her or anything because they knew that she was a fine person.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She was.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And she had known this Chinese when she got there. I don't know how she got into anything but she knew him and he was a gardener and she'd go and talk garden to him. She was a gardener too at heart. And so he would come with vegetables. So she saw him coming one day when I was there and she said you go to the door somebody's coming. And she said don't you dare get scared of anybody that's there. No. She said you just see what he wants. And I went to the door but I took a peek out and I saw it was a Chinese. But I'd heard him talk about this Chinese gardener and he had the two the pole over his shoulder with two baskets on each side. And of course I didn't know it at the time. I had nobody told me about it but you know that they don't say they're ours. Can't say they are. And so he said Missy lead and I said she's here.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: It's her house.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Missy lead. He said I got. Oh I don't know what he did save her vegetables. I forgot what he said for vegetables. I'm not sure about that. He said Missy lead. Nice fleshy blue Bobby. Nice fleshy you telly.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: You telly.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I said yes I'll tell her. And I said he says he has nice fresh rhubarb. Well I didn't know. I guess I didn't know what fresh mint either because I was a little girl in. But I knew it was rhubarb because rhubarb was loose in his basket. Big basket out. And he said nicey just for Missy lead. Her name is Reed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Mrs. Reed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: So Mrs. Reed came and he had rhubarb and he told her the gooseberries would soon be ready and he had enough for her pie. And a cup full measured out in a little sack and a cup full of enough for her pie. So Missy lead. That was free. She paid for the rhubarb and something. A radish. She had radishes and onions and she took some vegetables from him. So then later on of course after that I always wanted to go see Jean. Jean Chang I think his name was. His first name was Jean. The boys always called him. Hello Jean. I think it was Chang.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But Gilbert and I were always singing the next one because his chant was like a song to us. It was a...

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: casino.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Potato, tomato, sling beans and slabbage. Nice fleshy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And so he... everybody around there knew him and they'd always holler, hello Jean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you Jean?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Hello Jean. Everybody had accepted him there. Now in the early days a few had come into Moscow but not many. One got settled down in Kendrick and Papa said he was nice. He raised geese and he was a nice good fellow and he'd pick those geese and Papa said he never saw geese picked nicer. Now I never saw any of these geese. They could call a dozen of them Jean whether that was her name or not. But in Moscow there was one came in here and did the laundry. He'd drifted in after these little miners. Of course they'd have to have a laundry month up there and a gold digging. And they were treated nicely. But as far as I know I don't know of any mistreatment of the Chinese. But this Chinaman, I think I wrote that down in my notes about the Fourth of July celebration and the big fine concession that was built up by some group here in town. And they had a barker and he was dressed in white and black stand up hat and a coat you know with long tail coat red, white and blue. Oh he was really Uncle Sam. Uncle Sam was bobbing up and down and calling out all the things. And then just as he could finish taking breath, Jean Chinaman right across the street in the little bit of a place that was his laundry right out in front. And oh he had good at that time there was no bar on firecrackers. And he'd have fine ones and pretty pictures. I went from China. And he'd say and then of course he had lemonade and all that. He'd say smiling in the basement, you're so same here and lots cheapy.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: That's all he said and he did a thriving business. Now I didn't see that. That was before my day but you see my husband lived in Moscow as a little boy and he was tickled to death. And they always figured they'd buy everything. They bought from Jean Chinaman.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And then we never had Indians. The only Indians that I ever got a chance at were those that came up the ridge. In the summertime those that went up to Moscow to dig camos roots and those that went out huckleberrying. And they always came through as a few loads and I just got so I waved at them and that was all I was doing. But they very soon stopped. Once in a while I think they did stop once at our place that I remember up. But I suppose they could see that Mama was on the friend land at home. But if I was out and they saw me and they always waved at me and I waved at them. And I am sure that I never had and it never got it talked to me either. Not successfully. I heard about it but it never took effect. Any kind of prejudice against any race or any culture or anything. I've always been curious. I want to know about them and I want to know what it is because I figure that early in life I read that book, History. Eggleston's I think it was and he said the first thing he says is that the real American has not yet been born.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he goes on to say why?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Because he's a composite being. He's made up of this, that and the other thing and he goes on to tell how many things have come into the makeup of the American. And he goes on and he says finally we will have the Negro and the Indian and all those.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I have been interested in asking about that because I feel that you have an impartial perspective and I'm really interested in what people, what the general attitudes were as you perceive them.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: That's what you've really been telling me most. Well there's an awful lot of prejudice about Negroes. I was very 40 years before I discovered that my husband was prejudiced. Now he wasn't prejudiced this way. He'd speak to the Negroes but he wanted them to understand that he was superior. I didn't realize that. I don't. I don't feel that at all. There's a Negro family here in town that settles and poor Jean settle. I've forgotten what got the matter but he got hurt, injured and he had to have his legs amputated. And now he's in a wheelchair. He has, they've gotten legs for him. But he's very popular. And he was made commander of the American region. And my husband had a fit when he heard that. And then I had another one because my husband was saying that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well why not?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I said he's been a member for a long time and he's one of the best members they have. He attends regular aid. And I said everybody here likes Jean Settle. I never heard anybody that didn't and I sure like him. I like his wife. Well I had a fit about that. But then after that, that broke the ice. He got to telling me. And so then my mother used to sing a song that she unconsciously had picked up and was saying, I'm sorry, they had been around a lot of Negroes. And the Negroes lived right close to them and this old Aunt Chloe, they called them Grand Chloe. Old Aunt Chloe used to come to their place and just love my grandmother no wonder. Everybody loved her and I'm sure my grandmother wasn't prejudiced. I'll bet you anything. I wasn't. She was nice to that Chinaman. My mother, grandmother died when I was 14. And I was made the poorer for it. But I don't know because I'd never thought about taking out her. I got up with my grandmother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the song that you wrote to Mother and Mother?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, it was an old, dark hymn, I guess, that they crooned babies. And Mama had sung that. I'd heard her not too many times but I'd heard her. She'd been awfully prejudiced. She would never have sung that song, I agree with all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the song was?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Yes, it was. I remember some of it. I don't remember all of it. Go to sleep, you little pick and any. Underneath the southern summer sun. Slumber on the bosom of your own, mammy jenny. Bear, bear fox, I'll get you if you don't. That was one verse. I remember that one. I did know another one but I can't think of that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: But that was it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Go to sleep, you little pick and any. Underneath the southern summer sun. Slumber on the bosom of your own, mammy jenny. Bear fox, I'll get you if you don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: White, he said, do you realize if you're comparing yourself to an old Negro wench?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I said, don't call her a Negro wench. She's a Negro woman. Just as much as a woman as any white woman you've ever seen her possibly. A lot more than a lot of them. Maybe less than some, but more than others. I've never heard of such a thing. He said, well it seems to me that you were awful slow in making your ideas known about Negroes. We just had an upset about Jean Settle. Jean, and that was what was the matter. And I said, Paul, I did not know you were, you were prejudiced. He said, I'm not prejudiced. He said, I just want Negroes to keep you in the places where I'll keep mine. He said, I don't believe in mistreating an eagle. And he wouldn't either. He said, I wouldn't mistreat a Negro.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: This makes me curious about the attitudes towards Jewish people, which I've never thought of asking anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what were the attitudes towards, let's say, like Durnham and Coffman and the store?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Well, they had wealth enough that they commanded respect among the people down in Moscow. I know that. And I've asked about it, about people. Of course, you see, I wasn't old enough to know then. That is, I didn't know they were Jews. I didn't know Jews are any different than any other people. This business about knowing about Jews, I didn't know for a long time. Because it never came up. So there wasn't much talk about it. There wasn't much talk about it. No, there wasn't. There wasn't talk about it when I was a child. But the talk came later. And then I found out that in the East, they were terribly prejudiced against Jews. And my mother told me, my mother said that, I said, I read some book, I don't know. Maybe I read Shylock, I read the American Adventist and talked about Shylock, who is a great shining example of a Jew that has been taken up a lot. And I've heard it talked a lot. Maybe it was that. Seems to me that was a little bit advanced for me to be talking about at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But anyway, I said, why?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Why in the world is it that, oh yes, I'll tell you, I got to read it to Sir Walter Scott pretty early. That's where it was, you know. Oh, it's one of the books, Ivadon maybe, Rebecca, I don't know whether that was Ivadon or the Talisman, where Rebecca and Rowena were both in love with the same men, or he was in love with the both women, I guess that was it. But when he went back to England, of course, the fair haired one, he remembered her. She was his old love. After I got older and thought about that book, I thought it wasn't such a nice book, quite a business ad. He flirting around with so many. But anyway, there was that choice to be made between the English girl and the other girl. But he felt hardly sorry about Rowena, but she held her head up high and she wouldn't leave her people, she said, for anybody else. She would stay with the Jews when her father was... They looked down upon him and she couldn't see why because they came to him for money and the only way he could get it was to resort to the law. And that was the only way he could get the money back because they called him a dog and everything else. Dog and son of a dog, they didn't say the other word, but they'd say son of a dog. Now that wasn't some of Sir Waterscott's books, I remember that. And that came up for me to talk about to a few, but in general it wasn't a general topic of conversation. But later on, down here in town, there's a... some martins have a tire shop or a shop, an auto tire shop I think it is. And Evans, I didn't know that they were Jews or anything else. They're just like anybody else. And I didn't even know it. But one time at the... I belonged to the A .U .W. which is the Association of University Women because they had it on the campus and I don't know, I guess it was the year I graduated, I was doing a paper of some kind. And I joined it and I belonged ever since.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And they didn't have any prejudices like some of these little lodges do. They don't even have some of the lodges, you know, discriminate against people who belong to the order of Rebecca. No, it's the Rebecca's that don't want any Indians or any Negroes in there. And I understood the Mason Lodge didn't want any. And well, Mrs. Martin and I happened to bring that up. We were discussing literature. Oh, I was in a literary club. It was a branch of the A .U .W. but we were at a literary club. We have one organized within a club.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And oh, she said that this was a book. Somebody read the book and reported it. And she said it's a book on racial prejudice. Well, no, it was prejudice. It's a book on prejudice against the Jews and how the Jews were made to suffer in so many places. And I said, for the life of me, I can't understand why. And well, she said, you'd understand why quick enough if you were a Jew and you went into some big hotel back in Chicago or some of the other big cities, she said. And they tell you, you couldn't stay overnight there because you were a Jew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how in the world can they tell? How can they tell you're a Jew?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: She said, I'm not ashamed. I've not done anything to hide it. Well, I said, you don't wear any. It wizened on your clothing. Oh, yes, she said, I always wear the Star of David. She said, she made that remark. Well, I said, good night. I wouldn't look for the Star of David on anybody. And I said, besides, we've incorporated David in our Sunday school and study about David and all that sort of thing and regarding those patriarch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What in the world is the matter?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Now, that's my attitude. I've never been able to understand it. And as I said the other day, talking with you, I did get a little clarification on some of the Jewish matters. I never had clarified. But I haven't had that clarified why a Jew should be discriminated because of that. I can't the life of me. No more reason than anybody. Than anybody. Nor a Catholic. Of course, Catholics, a lot of people didn't want to have anything to do with the Catholics. Truth, the matter was the Irish, they were down on American Ridge. There were a lot of people that didn't want to weren't too happy about the Irish coming up to the church there on American Ridge because they were Catholics. And I heard a man say was Mr. Harlan. He didn't want his kids marrying a Catholic. Well, they didn't marry a Catholic. But he also said that he wasn't going to let him go to Troy to the dances now that all those Swedes had come in to town. All those Swedes and Norwegians were just flocking to the dance halls. They're great dancers. All those that had been up to Nora. I believe he said that it wasn't that had been to Nora were coming in. He said, first thing he knew, some of his kids would be marrying, marrying Swedes and Norwegians. And I said, I bet they will. I was a little girl and I was up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, oh, you think so? Are you going to marry one?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I don't know. I said, I haven't thought about it. I don't know who I'm going to marry. But I didn't marry the Swedes. A couple of his girls married Swedes. All right. You bet they did. They were brought up in this kind of a. But I thought it was funny. This is something else. A little side fight. I lived long enough to hear him say he lived long enough. He says, you know, the best son -in -law I've got is that Norwegians. That's Mary Dade. He said he's the best one of the whole lot and he had eight children. I heard him say that. He had changed his mind. He was a Democratic man and really a better thinker than most of the people here. And he would give many a man something to eat ahead of anybody else on the ridge. He was a generous man. Now, and still, he had that one little thing that I know wrong that I heard. And still, I don't think he ever showed it. I think he treated people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think Al Roberts's attitude was?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: Just briefly, I mean, Al Roberts, you said that he mixed with everybody. He did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And do you feel that he was not prejudiced?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: I know he ate breakfast with Joe, Uncle Joe. I called him Uncle Joe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He called Joe Welles Uncle Joe?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: He did.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson
words: And I'm sure there wasn't any of that in Al Roberts. I'm sure there wasn't. But in Manfred Harrown, that was the sweetest Norwegian and I don't know. I did hear him say that. I was a child and I heard him say it. But I don't think he advertised it because it didn't become talked about. So I don't think he tried to influence people.
